explanations: The optative expresses mainly probability and advice, and ideas such as those conveyed by if, might, would, etc. It is formed by adding -eyya to the verbal base before terminations.
It should be observed that the terminations of the second and first persons are similar to those of the present tense.

examples.pali: Sace so bhattaṃ paceyya, ahaṃ bhuñjeyyāmi.
examples.english: If he would cook rice I will eat.

examples.pali: Sace tvaṃ iccheyyāsi, ahaṃ coraṃ puccheyyāmi.
examples.english: If you would like, I will question the thief.

examples.pali: Yadi ahaṃ nagare vihareyyāmi, so pi nagaraṃ āgaccheyya.
examples.english: If I dwell in the city, he too would come to the city.

examples.pali: Sace te bhattaṃ paceyyuṃ, mayaṃ bhuñjeyyāma.
examples.english: If they cook rice we will eat.

examples.pali: Sace tumhe iccheyyātha, mayaṃ core puccheyyāma.
examples.english: If you so wish, we will question the thieves.

examples.pali: Yadi mayaṃ nagare vihareyyāma, te pi nagaraṃ āgaccheyyuṃ.
examples.english: If we dwell in the city, they too will come to the city.
